FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study; participants are 9-11 year old children from the ABCD cohort.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning framework with ABCD data to identify factors associated with fluid cognitive functioning (FCF).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen media activity measured with multiple specific modalities; methods indicate composite includes phone/SM items (texting, social media, video chat, YouTube). Paired with FCF (fluid cognitive functioning - individual-trait-level cognitive outcome). ML models report variable importance estimates for screen media activity as predictor of FCF.

FT_IC3.evidence: ML feature importance/variable importance values reported for screen media activity and its association with FCF; stacked model performance and VI across multiple base learners quantified.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative feature importance estimates reported; partial dependence plots and model importance values provided showing relationship between screen media and FCF.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline assessment only, not longitudinal comparison); FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs where SM is DV-only.
notes: ML-based study using variable importance metrics rather than traditional regression coefficients; all three screen media activity variables identified as contributors to FCF model.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10) and 1-year follow-up (2017-2019, ages 10-11).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis with logistic regression examining associations between baseline screen time and binge-eating disorder at follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article (International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identifies multiple phone/SM-specific estimates: social networking aOR 1.62 (95% CI 1.18-2.22), texting aOR 1.40 (95% CI 1.08-1.82), TV/movies aOR 1.39 (95% CI 1.14-1.69). All paired with binge-eating disorder (individual-trait mental health outcome based on KSADS-5 DSM-5 criteria). Also reports total screen time and other modalities.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported for all screen time modalities and total screen time in relation to binge-eating disorder.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results (aOR and 95% CI) reported in Table 2 for SM/phone variables paired with binge-eating disorder outcome.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline screen time predicts 1-year follow-up binge-eating disorder). Binge-eating disorder is DV in every analysis. Screen time/SM appears only as IV (exposure), never as DV in any cross-sectional or longitudinal analysis. All three FT-EC7 conditions met; cross-sectional carve-out does not apply (no cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis present).
notes: Paper specifically examines prospective associations between baseline screen time modalities and incident binge-eating disorder; uses logistic regression with multiple covariates.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes prospective cohort data from the U.S. ABCD Study; baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10) and 1-year follow-up (2017-2019, ages 10-11) assessments.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis with multiple linear regression examining associations between baseline screen time behaviors and BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Multiple phone/SM-specific estimates identified: texting B=0.92 (95% CI 0.29-1.55), video chat B=0.72 (95% CI 0.09-1.36), video games B=0.42 (95% CI 0.06-0.78). All paired with BMI percentile (physical health/individual-trait outcome). Also reports total screen time and TV/movies, YouTube associations.

FT_IC3.evidence: B coefficients with 95% confidence intervals reported for all screen time modalities in relation to BMI percentile outcome in fully adjusted linear regression models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results (B coefficients and 95% CI) reported in Table 2 for screen time variables paired with BMI percentile outcome.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline screen time predicts 1-year follow-up BMI percentile). BMI percentile is DV in every analysis. Screen time/SM appears only as IV (exposure), never as DV in any cross-sectional or longitudinal analysis. All three FT-EC7 conditions met; cross-sectional carve-out does not apply.
notes: Paper examines prospective associations between contemporary screen time modalities and BMI percentile using multiple linear regression; secondary covariates include depression and binge-eating disorder.
